macro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Principle: Retaining Tree Ownership Preserves Land Rights

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rav Huna's Statement and the Dispute Between Rashbam and Tosafot on Ownership
Application of Rav Huna's Principle According to the Rabbis (Be'ayin Ra'ah) vs. Rabbi Akiva (Be'ayin Yafah)

macro_segments.title: Challenging Rav Huna via the Mishnah's Hekdesh Case: Trees Become Sacred Without Underlying Land

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Case: Sanctifying a Field and Which Components Transfer to Hekdesh
Rabbi Shimon's Position: Hekdesh Receives Tree but Not Underlying Land
Gemara's Resolution: Rav Huna's Statement Applies Only to the Rabbis (Be'ayin Ra'ah), Not to Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: Can Rabbi Shimon Follow Rabbi Akiva? Examining the Baraita on Proper Tree Distribution

micro_segments.title: Question: How Can Rabbi Shimon Follow Rabbi Akiva (Be'ayin Yafah) When He Is Akiva's Student?
Baraita: Three Trees Distributed Properly Include Underlying Land in Hekdesh
Proper Distribution Defined: Ten Trees per Beis Se'ah; Spacing Requirements
When Trees Are Improperly Distributed or Sanctified Individually: Land Does Not Transfer

macro_segments.title: Baraita Analysis: Redemption Values When Trees and Land Are Sanctified Separately or Together

micro_segments.title: Fixed vs. Market Rate: Sanctifying Field as Unit (Fixed Torah Rate) vs. Components Separately (Market Rates)
Question: Whose Position Does the Baraita Represent? Not Rabbi Akiva, But the Rabbis

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon According to the Rabbis (Be'ayin Ra'ah): Stingy Sanctification Excludes Land

micro_segments.title: Establishing That Rabbi Shimon in the Baraita Follows the Rabbis' Stingy Model (Be'ayin Ra'ah)
Contradiction: If Rabbi Shimon Follows Stingy Model, Why Does Tree Become Sacred When Field Is Sanctified?
Resolution: Rabbi Shimon's Own Position vs. His Explanation Using the Rabbis' Logic

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Orientation to Redemption Time vs. Sanctification Time: The Purchased Field Case

micro_segments.title: Problem: Why Does the Baraita Treat Two-Stage Sanctification as Two Separate Redemptions If Rabbi Shimon Looks at Redemption Time?
Baraita: Son Buys Field from Father, Sanctifies It, Then Father Dies
Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon: Field Treated as Ancestral (Sdeh Achuzah) at Time of Redemption, Not Sanctification
Rabbi Meir's Objection: Only If Father Died Before Sanctification Do We Treat as Ancestral Field
Gemara's Explanation: Rabbi Shimon's Position Based on Textual Reading, Not General Principle

macro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's Clarification: Rabbi Shimon Generally Follows Sanctification Time, Not Redemption Time

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman: In General, Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon Do Not Follow Redemption Time
The Text Forces Their Hand: The Phrase 'Asher Lo Misedeh Achuzato' Compels Alternative Reading

macro_segments.title: Dual-Status Objects: Carob Trees and Sheaves (Omerim) as Both Individual Units and Land/Collective

micro_segments.title: Carob and Sycamore: Treated as Tree (Torat Ilan) When Sold with Other Trees, and as Separate Land (Torat Karka) When Field Is Sold
Large Sheaf (Omer with Two Sa'ah): Treated as Sheaf (Torat Omer) and as Pile (Torat Gadish) Simultaneously
Application: How Dual Status Affects Shikhchah (Forgotten Produce) Laws

macro_segments.title: Attributing Positions on Carob and Sycamore: Rabbi Menachem bar Rabbi Yossi as Follower of Rabbi Shimon

micro_segments.title: Source Attribution: Carob and Sycamore Debate Attributed to Rabbi Menachem bar Rabbi Yossi vs. Rabbis, Likely Reflecting Rabbi Shimon vs. Rabbis
